Article.ArticleTitle: Severe Rhabdomyolysis With Acute Kidney Injury Triggered by Influenza A and Strenuous Exercise in a Healthy Adolescent: A Dual-Hit Mechanism.

Abstract.AbstractText: Rhabdomyolysis is a serious clinical condition often associated with serious complications and can be life-threatening in nature. The following is a case report of a healthy 17-year-old male adolescent who presented with rhabdomyolysis due to a dual hit of Influenza A and excessive physical exertion. Acute onset of myalgia, weakness in the lower extremities, and dark urine warranted his hospitalization in the intensive care unit for critical monitoring and investigation. The laboratory findings indicated a high creatine kinase level of more than 60,000 U/L, severe metabolic acidosis, hyperkalemia, and renal failure. Despite aggressive fluid resuscitation, persistent anuria and metabolic derangements warranted renal replacement therapy with continuous veno-venous hemofiltration followed by intermittent hemodialysis, resulting in progressive recovery of renal function and clinical improvement. This case highlights the importance of recognizing rhabdomyolysis in patients presenting with acute neuromuscular symptoms after febrile illness and strenuous exercise, emphasizing the need for early diagnosis and prompt supportive management to prevent irreversible renal injury.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Human subjects: Informed consent for treatment and open access publication was obtained or waived by all participants in this study. Conflicts of interest: In compliance with the ICMJE uniform disclosure form, all authors declare the following: Payment/services info: All authors have declared that no financial support was received from any organization for the submitted work. Financial relationships: All authors have declared that they have no financial relationships at present or within the previous three years with any organizations that might have an interest in the submitted work. Other relationships: All authors have declared that there are no other relationships or activities that could appear to have influenced the submitted work.

Reference.Citation: Bench-to-bedside review: rhabdomyolysis -- an overview for clinicians. Huerta-Alardín AL, Varon J, Marik PE. Crit Care. 2005;9:158–169.
Rhabdomyolysis: pathogenesis, diagnosis, and treatment. Torres PA, Helmstetter JA, Kaye AM, Kaye AD. https://www.ochsnerjournal.org/content/15/1/58. Ochsner J. 2015;15:58–69.
Advances in rhabdomyolysis: a review of pathogenesis, diagnosis, and treatment. Yang BF, Li D, Liu CL, et al. Chin J Traumatol. 2026;29:21–31.
Exercise-induced rhabdomyolysis: a case report and literature review. Al Badi A, Al Rasbi S, Alalawi AM. Cureus. 2020;12:0.

Validation of digital visual analog scale pain scoring with a traditional paper-based visual analog scale in adults. Delgado DA, Lambert BS, Boutris N, McCulloch PC, Robbins AB, Moreno MR, Harris JD. J Am Acad Orthop Surg Glob Res Rev. 2018;2:0.
Jain S, Margetis K, Iverson LM. StatPearls [Internet] Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025. Glasgow coma scale.
Rhabdomyolysis: review of the literature. Zutt R, van der Kooi AJ, Linthorst GE, Wanders RJ, de Visser M. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nmd.2014.05.005. Neuromuscul Disord. 2014;24:651–659.
Beyond muscle destruction: a systematic review of rhabdomyolysis for clinical practice. Chavez LO, Leon M, Einav S, Varon J. Crit Care. 2016;20:135.
The syndrome of rhabdomyolysis: complications and treatment. Chatzizisis YS, Misirli G, Hatzitolios AI, Giannoglou GD. Eur J Intern Med. 2008;19:568–574.
Rhabdomyolysis: a genetic perspective. Scalco RS, Gardiner AR, Pitceathly RD, et al. Orphanet J Rare Dis. 2015;10:51.

Acute renal failure due to rhabdomyolysis associated with echovirus 9 infection: a case report and review of literature. Tanaka T, Takada T, Takagi D, Takeyama N, Kitazawa Y. Jpn J Med. 1989;28:237–242.
Rhabdomyolysis secondary to influenza a infection: a case report and review of the literature. Fadila MF, Wool KJ. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4382767/ N Am J Med Sci. 2015;7:122–124.
Rhabdomyolysis and pandemic (H1N1) 2009 pneumonia in adult. Gutierrez RL, Ellis MW, Decker CF. Emerg Infect Dis. 2010;16:565.
Rhabdomyolysis following pandemic influenza A (H1N1) infection. Chen SC, Liu KS, Chang HR, Lee YT, Chen CC, Lee MC. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20739729/ Neth J Med. 2010;68:317–319.
Elevation of creatine kinase is associated with worse outcomes in 2009 pH1N1 influenza A infection. Borgatta B, Pérez M, Rello J, et al. Intensive Care Med. 2012;38:1152–1161.
When exercise causes exertional rhabdomyolysis. Furman J. JAAPA. 2015;28:38–43.
Rhabdomyolysis: patterns, circumstances, and outcomes of patients presenting to the emergency department. Knafl EG, Hughes JA, Dimeski G, Eley R. Ochsner J. 2018;18:215–221.
Exercise-induced rhabdomyolysis causing acute kidney injury: a potential threat to gym lovers. Kumar R, Kumar S, Kumar A, Kumar D, Kumar V. Cureus. 2022;14:0.
Acute rhabdomyolysis and inflammation. Hamel Y, Mamoune A, Mauvais FX, et al. J Inherit Metab Dis. 2015;38:621–628.
Inflammation balance in skeletal muscle damage and repair. Tu H, Li YL. Front Immunol. 2023;14:1133355.
Diagnostic evaluation of rhabdomyolysis. Nance JR, Mammen AL. Muscle Nerve. 2015;51:793–810.
